deepExplanation: Syntax: record Point(int x, int y) {}. Auto-generated: Point(int x, int y) constructor, int x() getter, int y() getter, equals(), hashCode(), toString(). Records can: implement interfaces, have static fields/methods, have custom constructors (compact constructor for validation), add instance methods. Records cannot: extend other classes (implicitly extend Record), have instance fields other than components, be abstract. Ideal for: DTOs, Value Objects, API request/response models, database projections.
followup: When would you NOT use a record?
realWorldUsage: Replace @Data Lombok DTOs with records: record UserDTO(String name, String email, int age) {} — zero boilerplate, immutable, perfect for REST API request/response models. Compact constructor for validation: record UserDTO(String name, String email) { UserDTO { if(email == null) throw new IllegalArgumentException("email required"); } }. Don't use records for JPA entities — JPA requires mutable objects with a no-arg constructor.

title: What are virtual threads in Java 21? How do they change the concurrency model?
answer: Virtual threads (Project Loom) are lightweight threads managed by the JVM, not the OS. You can create millions of virtual threads — each blocking call parks the virtual thread and frees the OS thread for other work. Transforms the threading model from "thread per request" back to a viable pattern.
deepExplanation: Problem with platform (OS) threads: thread creation is expensive (~1MB stack), 10,000 threads ≈ 10GB RAM. Traditional solution: reactive programming (WebFlux, RxJava) — complex, hard to debug, callback hell. Virtual thread solution: Java manages millions of lightweight threads mapped onto a small pool of OS "carrier threads." When a virtual thread does blocking I/O, it unmounts from the carrier thread — the carrier thread picks up another virtual thread. Result: high concurrency with simple synchronous code style. Spring Boot 3.2+: spring.threads.virtual.enabled=true — all HTTP handling uses virtual threads.
followup: What are the limitations of virtual threads?
realWorldUsage: Limitations: (1) Synchronized blocks can "pin" the virtual thread to the carrier thread (defeats the purpose) — use ReentrantLock instead. (2) ThreadLocal with large objects can cause memory pressure with millions of threads. (3) Not a silver bullet for CPU-bound work — only helps I/O-bound blocking. Spring WebMVC with virtual threads = simple code + reactive-level throughput for I/O-bound APIs. No need to migrate to WebFlux just for scalability.

title: How do you implement rate limiting in a Spring Boot REST API?
answer: Rate limiting restricts how often a client can call your API. Common approaches: (1) API Gateway level (AWS API Gateway, Kong). (2) Filter/Interceptor level using Bucket4j or Resilience4j RateLimiter. (3) Redis-backed distributed rate limiting (for multi-instance deployments).
deepExplanation: Bucket4j approach: token bucket algorithm — each request consumes a token, tokens refill at a fixed rate. @RequestMapping + Bucket4j filter: if bucket.tryConsume(1) → proceed; else → 429 Too Many Requests with Retry-After header. For distributed (multiple app instances): use Redis-backed Bucket4j (each instance shares the same token bucket in Redis via atomic operations). Include X-RateLimit-Remaining and X-RateLimit-Reset headers in responses. Resilience4j RateLimiter: @RateLimiter(name="myApi") annotation — simpler for per-method limits.
followup: What is the difference between rate limiting and throttling?
realWorldUsage: Per-user rate limiting: extract user ID from JWT in filter, use userId as bucket key in Redis. Free plan: 100 requests/minute. Pro plan: 1000 requests/minute. When bucket exhausted, return 429 with {"error": "Rate limit exceeded", "retryAfter": 45}. Always return Retry-After header — clients can implement smart retry instead of hammering your API.

title: How do you secure a microservices architecture?
answer: To secure microservices, simultaneously implement security unit tests like SAST (Static Analysis Security Testing) and DAST (Dynamic Analysis Security Testing). You should also encrypt and protect secrets, and use an API Gateway with rate limiting to slow down attackers.
deepExplanation: SAST works from the inside, scanning your application's code and imported libraries for vulnerabilities. DAST works from the outside, mimicking malicious attacks to identify vulnerabilities. OAuth 2.0 is recommended for identity and access management everywhere.
followup: How does rate limiting protect an API?
realWorldUsage: Rate limiting ensures that if someone tries to attack your API with hundreds of requests, they can only make a limited number of attempts (e.g., one or two attempts per second). This is usually implemented via an API gateway.

title: What is an Auth Guard in Angular and how is it used?
answer: Auth Guard is used to protect the routes from unauthorized access in an Angular application.
deepExplanation: Auth guards provide lifecycle events like CanActivate. The CanActivate method has to return true to allow access to the page. If it returns false, the user cannot access the page.
followup: How do you generate an Auth Guard using the Angular CLI?
realWorldUsage: You use the command `ng generate guard` to create it. Inside the CanActivate method, you typically call an authentication service (e.g., `isAuthenticatedService.getAuthState()`) to check if the current user is valid.

title: What is a Two-Phase Commit (2PC) in distributed systems?
answer: Two-phase commit in Java carries out an update in two phases: the prepare phase and the commit phase.
deepExplanation: In the prepare phase, the coordinator asks each node if it is able to carry out the update, and each node acquires what it needs to assure it can commit. Once each node promises it can commit, the second phase begins where the coordinator actually carries it out. If any node is unable to make that promise, the coordinator tells all nodes to rollback and the transaction is aborted.
followup: What is a modern alternative to 2PC in Microservices?
realWorldUsage: In microservices, the Saga architecture pattern is often preferred over 2PC. It provides transaction management using a sequence of local transactions, and if one fails, compensating transactions run to undo the previously completed work.

title: Explain the Hibernate Entity Lifecycle States.
answer: There are 4 states in the Hibernate lifecycle: Transient state, Persistent state, Detached state, and Removed state.
deepExplanation: Transient: Object is instantiated using the "new" operator but not connected to any hibernate session. Persistent: Object is connected with the hibernate session and represents one row in the DB table; changes to data are automatically detected and updated. Detached: The session is closed or cleared; changes will not affect the database table unless reconnected. Removed: The entity object is deleted from the database using the delete() operation.
followup: How do you move an object from Detached back to Persistent?
realWorldUsage: To reconnect a detached object to a new hibernate session, you must use methods like merge(), update(), load(), or refresh().

title: Explain First-level and Second-level caching in Hibernate.
answer: Caching improves application performance by pooling objects in cache when the same data must be fetched multiple times. There are two main types: First-level cache and Second-level cache.
deepExplanation: First-level cache is enabled by default and is maintained by the Session object. Data held by one session object is not accessible to the entire application or other sessions. Second-level cache is disabled by default, is maintained by the SessionFactory object, and its data is accessible to all sessions across the entire application.
followup: What are some vendors that provide Second-level cache implementations?
realWorldUsage: Different vendors provide implementations for the second-level cache, such as EH Cache, OS Cache, Swarm Cache, and JBoss Cache.

title: What are the SOLID principles in Java?
answer: SOLID principles include: Single Responsibility, Open-Closed, Liskov Substitution, Interface Segregation, and Dependency Inversion.
deepExplanation: Single Responsibility: A class should have only one reason to change. Open-Closed: Classes should be open for extension and closed to modification. Liskov Substitution: Subclasses should be substitutable for their base classes. Interface Segregation: Many client-specific interfaces are better than one general-purpose interface. Dependency Inversion: Classes should depend upon interfaces or abstract classes instead of concrete classes and functions.
followup: What does the Open-Closed principle mean in practice?
realWorldUsage: It means we should be able to add new functionality without touching the existing code for the class.

title: What are the categories of Design Patterns in Java? Give examples.
answer: Java design patterns are divided into three categories: Creational, Structural, and Behavioural.
deepExplanation: Creational patterns (Singleton, Factory, Abstract Factory, Builder, Prototype) provide solutions to instantiate an object in the best possible way. Structural patterns (Adapter, Composite, Proxy, Flyweight, Facade, Bridge, Decorator) provide ways to create a class structure using inheritance and composition. Behavioural patterns (Template, Mediator, Chain of Responsibility, Observer, Strategy, Command, State, Visitor) provide solutions for better interaction between objects and loose-coupling.
followup: When would you use the Factory pattern?
realWorldUsage: The Factory Pattern is used when we have a superclass with multiple subclasses and, based on input, we need to return one of the subclasses. This takes the responsibility of instantiation from the client program to the factory class.

title: What is a JWT and what is its internal structure?
answer: JWT (JSON Web Token) is an open standard for securely transmitting information between parties as a JSON object. It consists of three Base64-URL encoded parts separated by dots: Header, Payload, and Signature.
deepExplanation: The Header contains the algorithm (e.g., HS256 or RS256) and token type. The Payload contains "claims" (data like userId, roles, expiration). The Signature is created using the encoded header, encoded payload, a secret key, and the specified algorithm. Because it is mathematically signed, the server can verify if the payload has been tampered with.
followup: Is the payload of a JWT encrypted?
realWorldUsage: No, the payload is only Base64 encoded, meaning anyone who intercepts the token can decode and read the data. Never put sensitive information like passwords or credit card numbers inside a JWT payload.

title: What is the difference between OAuth 2.0 and JWT?
answer: OAuth 2.0 is an authorization framework (a protocol) that allows a user to grant a third-party application access to their resources. JWT is simply a token format.
deepExplanation: OAuth 2.0 defines the "dance" (the flows, like Authorization Code Flow or Client Credentials Flow) to securely acquire an Access Token. It does not dictate what the token looks like. In modern architectures, the Access Token issued by an OAuth 2.0 Authorization Server is very often formatted as a JWT, but it could theoretically be a random opaque string mapped to a database.
followup: What is the purpose of a Refresh Token in OAuth 2.0?
realWorldUsage: Access tokens have short lifespans (e.g., 15 minutes) for security. When it expires, the frontend uses the longer-lived Refresh Token (which is kept secure and rarely transmitted) to request a new Access Token from the Auth Server without forcing the user to log in again.

title: How does the Spring Security Filter Chain work internally?
answer: Spring Security intercepts incoming HTTP requests using a chain of Servlet Filters (the SecurityFilterChain) before the request ever reaches the DispatcherServlet or your Controllers.
deepExplanation: Key filters include the UsernamePasswordAuthenticationFilter (for processing login forms) and BasicAuthenticationFilter. When authenticating, it creates an Authentication object and passes it to the AuthenticationManager. The Manager delegates to an AuthenticationProvider (like DaoAuthenticationProvider), which uses a UserDetailsService to load the user from the DB. Once authenticated, the Authentication object is stored in the SecurityContextHolder (which uses a ThreadLocal).
followup: How do you implement a custom JWT filter in Spring Security?
realWorldUsage: For a stateless API, you create a custom JwtRequestFilter extending OncePerRequestFilter. You place it before the UsernamePasswordAuthenticationFilter. It intercepts the request, extracts the "Authorization: Bearer <token>" header, validates the signature, extracts the user roles, and manually saves the Authentication object into the SecurityContextHolder.

title: Why are JWTs preferred for Microservices over traditional Session IDs?
answer: Traditional session IDs are stateful—they require the server to store session data in memory or a database. JWTs are stateless. All the necessary user data (identity, roles) is contained within the token itself.
deepExplanation: If Server A creates a JSESSIONID, and the load balancer routes the next request to Server B, Server B will reject the user unless you configure sticky sessions or a centralized Redis session store. With a JWT, Server B just needs the public key or shared secret to verify the token's signature mathematically. It doesn't need to query a database to know who the user is.
followup: What is the hardest problem to solve when using stateless JWTs?
realWorldUsage: Token Revocation. If a user logs out or their account is compromised, you cannot easily invalidate a stateless JWT before its expiration time because no server is tracking it. You usually have to build a "Token Blacklist" in Redis, which ironically introduces state back into your stateless architecture.

title: What is the difference between Authentication and Authorization?
answer: Authentication is verifying WHO the user is (e.g., checking a username and password). Authorization is verifying WHAT the authenticated user is allowed to do (e.g., checking if they have the "ADMIN" role to delete a user).
deepExplanation: In Spring Security, Authentication is handled by the AuthenticationManager. Authorization is handled later in the process, often using Method Security annotations like @PreAuthorize("hasRole('ADMIN')") on the controller or service methods.
realWorldUsage: A user logging in with correct credentials passes Authentication. If that regular user tries to hit the /api/admin/delete endpoint and gets a 403 Forbidden, they failed Authorization.

title: Explain CORS and CSRF. How do JWTs relate to CSRF?
answer: CORS (Cross-Origin Resource Sharing) is a browser restriction preventing a web page from making requests to a different domain. CSRF (Cross-Site Request Forgery) is an attack where a malicious site tricks a user's browser into executing an unwanted action on a trusted site where they are authenticated.
deepExplanation: Traditional web apps use cookies for sessions. Browsers automatically attach cookies to cross-site requests, making them vulnerable to CSRF. If your API is strictly stateless and relies on the frontend manually attaching a JWT to the HTTP "Authorization: Bearer" header, your application is inherently immune to CSRF because the browser won't automatically send the JWT.
followup: If JWTs prevent CSRF, why might a company still store a JWT in an HttpOnly cookie?
realWorldUsage: Storing a JWT in LocalStorage makes it vulnerable to XSS (Cross-Site Scripting) attacks—any malicious JavaScript can read LocalStorage and steal the token. Storing it in an HttpOnly cookie prevents XSS theft, but it re-introduces the CSRF vulnerability! In that case, you must re-enable Spring Security's CSRF protection.

title: How do you secure passwords in a Spring Boot application?
answer: Passwords should never be stored in plain text. You must use a strong, slow, salted hashing algorithm like BCrypt, Argon2, or PBKDF2. Spring Security provides PasswordEncoder interfaces for this.
deepExplanation: BCrypt is the standard. It automatically generates a random salt for every password and embeds the salt into the final hash string. Because it is computationally slow ("work factor"), it protects against brute-force and rainbow table attacks. You configure it by exposing a BCryptPasswordEncoder @Bean in your configuration class.
followup: Why is MD5 or SHA-256 no longer recommended for passwords?
realWorldUsage: MD5 and SHA-256 are designed to be extremely fast. A modern GPU can calculate billions of SHA-256 hashes per second, making them trivial to brute-force. BCrypt is intentionally designed to be slow, throttling the attacker's hardware.

title: Two Sum
shortSummary: Use a HashMap to store complement→index. O(n) single pass.
intro: Given an array of integers and a target, return indices of two numbers that add up to target.
intuition: For each number x, we need target-x. Store visited numbers in a map and check if the complement exists.
approach:
1. Create HashMap<Integer,Integer>.
2. For each nums[i]: compute complement = target-nums[i].
3. If map has complement → return [map.get(complement), i].
4. Else put nums[i]→i in map.
example: nums=[2,7,11,15], target=9 → at i=1 (val=7), complement=2 found at index 0 → [0,1]
edgeCases: Duplicate values like [3,3] target=6 — works because we check before putting
No solution — return empty (clarify with interviewer)
talkTrack: I ask: can we use same element twice? Is there always exactly one solution? Then explain: for each element check if its complement is already in the map. Classic space-for-time trade.
followups: What if sorted — can you do O(1) space with two pointers?

title: Valid Parentheses
shortSummary: Use a stack. Push open brackets, pop and validate on close brackets.
intro: Given a string of brackets (), [], {}, return true if every open bracket has a matching close bracket in correct order.
intuition: A stack mirrors the LIFO order of nested brackets. When we see a close bracket, the most recent open must match.
approach:
1. Stack<Character>.
2. For each char: if open → push. If close → if stack empty or top mismatches → false; else pop.
3. Return stack.isEmpty().
example: "({[]})" → push (, push {, push [ → see ] pop [, see } pop {, see ) pop ( → stack empty → true
edgeCases: Odd length string — can never be valid
Only close brackets — stack empty on first pop
Unmatched open at end — stack not empty
talkTrack: Stack is natural here: closing brackets must match the most recently unclosed open bracket — LIFO. Map each close to its expected open for clean lookup.

title: Merge Two Sorted Lists
shortSummary: Dummy head node. Compare heads of both lists, attach smaller, advance that pointer.
intro: Merge two sorted linked lists and return the merged sorted list.
approach:
1. dummy = new ListNode(-1), curr = dummy.
2. While both non-null: attach smaller, advance its pointer, advance curr.
3. Attach remaining non-null list.
4. Return dummy.next.
talkTrack: Dummy node eliminates head special-casing. Pick smaller node, attach, advance. At end just attach remaining tail — no need to iterate it.

title: Best Time to Buy and Sell Stock
shortSummary: Track min price so far. maxProfit = max(profit, price - minPrice).
intro: Given daily prices, find maximum profit from one buy before one sell.
approach:
1. minPrice = MAX_VALUE, maxProfit = 0.
2. For each price: update minPrice, update maxProfit = max(maxProfit, price - minPrice).
talkTrack: At each day: either this is the buy day (new min) or the sell day (compute profit vs historical min). Never need to look backward.

title: Valid Palindrome
shortSummary: Two pointers from both ends. Skip non-alphanumeric, compare lowercased.
intro: A string is palindrome if it reads same forward/backward, ignoring case and non-alphanumeric chars.
talkTrack: No need to create a cleaned string (O(n) space). Two pointers skip in-place. O(1) space.
followups: Valid Palindrome II — remove at most one char?

title: Invert Binary Tree
shortSummary: Swap left/right children at each node recursively. BFS with queue also works.
intro: Given the root of a binary tree, invert it (mirror) and return root.
talkTrack: Inverting a tree = swap children + invert each child. Elegant recursive definition. Mention BFS if they ask for iterative.
followups: Symmetric Tree — is it its own mirror?

title: Valid Anagram
shortSummary: int[26] char frequency. Increment for s, decrement for t. All zeros → true.
intro: Return true if t is an anagram of s (same chars, same count).
talkTrack: int[26] is perfect for ASCII lowercase. For Unicode: HashMap. Increment s, decrement t — any nonzero means mismatch.

title: Binary Search
shortSummary: Maintain [left,right]. Compare mid to target. Narrow half each step.
intro: Given sorted array and target, return its index or -1.
talkTrack: Key pitfall: use left+(right-left)/2 to avoid integer overflow. Loop condition left<=right for exact match.

title: Flood Fill
shortSummary: DFS from source. Change all connected same-color cells. Exit early if original == new color.
intro: Given an image (2D matrix), flood fill from (sr,sc) with new color.
edgeCases: Source already has new color — exit early to avoid infinite loop
talkTrack: Check orig==newColor first to avoid infinite recursion. DFS only visits same-color connected cells.

title: LCA of BST
shortSummary: Both < root → go left. Both > root → go right. Otherwise root is LCA.
intro: Find the lowest common ancestor of two nodes in a BST.
edgeCases: One node is ancestor of other
Root equals one of the nodes
talkTrack: BST ordering lets us skip one subtree at every step. First point where values split to different sides (or root equals one) is the LCA.

title: Balanced Binary Tree
shortSummary: DFS returning height. Propagate -1 as "unbalanced" signal when abs(left-right)>1.
intro: Determine if a binary tree is height-balanced (every node subtree heights differ by ≤1).
talkTrack: Naive O(n²) computes height separately per node. Bottom-up O(n) computes height and checks balance together. -1 sentinel short-circuits on first imbalance.
followups: Min nodes in balanced BST of height h?

title: Linked List Cycle
shortSummary: Floyd's tortoise and hare. Slow=1 step, fast=2. If they meet → cycle.
intro: Determine if a linked list has a cycle.
talkTrack: HashSet O(n) space vs Floyd O(1). Always use Floyd. If cycle length C, fast gains 1 step per iter, meets slow within C iters.

title: Implement Queue using Stacks
shortSummary: inbox stack for push. outbox stack for pop/peek. Transfer all inbox→outbox only when outbox empty.
intro: Implement FIFO queue using two LIFO stacks.
talkTrack: Each element moves inbox→outbox exactly once. Amortized O(1) pop. I say "amortized" explicitly.

title: First Bad Version
shortSummary: Binary search for first true in [F,F,F,T,T,T]. Use left<right, right=mid when bad.
intro: Find first bad version minimizing isBadVersion() API calls.
edgeCases: All good (guaranteed at least one bad)
talkTrack: left<right boundary search. When mid is bad, right=mid (not mid-1) because mid itself might be the first bad. Converges when left==right.
followups: Generalized binary search for first condition?

title: Ransom Note
shortSummary: Count magazine letters. Decrement for each ransomNote letter. Return false if any goes negative.
intro: Can ransomNote be built from magazine letters?
edgeCases: Chars in note not in magazine
talkTrack: Build character bank from magazine, spend it for ransomNote.

title: Climbing Stairs
shortSummary: dp[i] = dp[i-1]+dp[i-2]. Fibonacci. Optimize to O(1) space with two variables.
intro: Count ways to climb n stairs (1 or 2 steps at a time).
talkTrack: Recognize as Fibonacci. Explain bottom-up DP then space optimization to two variables.

title: Longest Palindrome
shortSummary: Sum (freq/2)*2 for all chars. Add 1 if any odd-frequency char exists (center).
intro: Find the length of longest palindrome buildable from given string's characters.
talkTrack: Count pairs. Even-freq contributes all. Odd-freq contributes freq-1 as pairs, one can be center.

title: Reverse Linked List
shortSummary: prev=null, curr=head. Save next, flip curr.next=prev, advance both.
intro: Reverse a singly linked list.
talkTrack: Walk through pointer steps: save next, flip pointer, advance. Recursive is elegant but O(n) stack space.

title: Majority Element
shortSummary: Boyer-Moore Voting: candidate+count. Count→0 means switch candidate. Survivor is majority.
intro: Find the element appearing more than n/2 times. Guaranteed to exist.
talkTrack: Think vote-cancellation: every majority vote cancelled by a minority still leaves net majority survivor.
followups: What if majority might not exist — verify?

title: Add Binary
shortSummary: Process right-to-left with carry. Append sum%2, carry=sum/2. Reverse result.
intro: Given two binary strings, return their sum as a binary string.
talkTrack: Same pattern as Add Two Numbers (linked list). Process from LSB, maintain carry.

title: Diameter of Binary Tree
shortSummary: DFS returns height. At each node: diameter = left+right. Track global max.
intro: Diameter = longest path between any two nodes. May not pass through root.
talkTrack: Key insight: longest path might be entirely within a subtree. Track global max as we compute heights bottom-up.

title: Middle of Linked List
shortSummary: Slow/fast pointers. Fast moves 2x. When fast ends, slow is at middle.
intro: Return the middle node of a linked list. If two middles, return the second.
talkTrack: Fast at 2x speed: when it ends, slow is exactly midpoint.

title: Maximum Depth of Binary Tree
shortSummary: 1 + max(depth(left), depth(right)). BFS counts levels.
intro: Return the maximum depth (root-to-leaf nodes count) of a binary tree.
talkTrack: Recursive definition is self-evident. For deep trees (stack overflow risk), use BFS.
followups: Count nodes in complete binary tree?

title: Contains Duplicate
shortSummary: HashSet.add() returns false if already present → duplicate found.
intro: Return true if any value appears at least twice.
talkTrack: add() returning false is an elegant O(1) duplicate check. Could also sort+scan for O(1) space.

title: Maximum Subarray
shortSummary: Kadane's: currSum = max(num, currSum+num). Track global max.
intro: Find contiguous subarray with largest sum (Kadane's Algorithm).
talkTrack: If running sum goes negative, it hurts future subarrays — restart from current element. Initialize both to nums[0] to handle all-negative.

title: Insert Interval
shortSummary: Add non-overlapping before, merge overlapping, add rest.
intro: Given sorted non-overlapping intervals and a new interval, insert and merge.
talkTrack: Three phases: copy non-overlapping prefix, merge overlapping (extend boundaries), copy suffix. Merge condition: intervals[i][0] <= newInterval[1].

title: 01 Matrix
shortSummary: Multi-source BFS from all 0s simultaneously. Cell distance = BFS level when first reached.
intro: Find distance of nearest 0 for each cell in a binary matrix.
talkTrack: Multi-source BFS is O(mn) vs O(m²n²) for single-source per cell. Expand from all zeroes like a "wave".

title: K Closest Points to Origin
shortSummary: Max-heap of size k. If new dist < heap top → remove max, add new. O(n log k).
intro: Return k closest points to origin.
talkTrack: Sort O(n log n), max-heap O(n log k) better when k<<n. Mention QuickSelect O(n) avg for best performance.

title: Longest Substring Without Repeating Characters
shortSummary: Sliding window: track last index of each char. Shrink left past duplicate.
intro: Find length of longest substring without repeating characters.
talkTrack: Store index not boolean — allows O(1) jump of left pointer past the duplicate.

title: 3Sum
shortSummary: Sort. Fix i, two pointers lo/hi. Skip duplicates at all three positions.
intro: Find all unique triplets summing to zero.
talkTrack: Sort enables two-pointer + duplicate skipping. Walk through duplicate-heavy example in interview.

title: Binary Tree Level Order Traversal
shortSummary: BFS. Snapshot queue size before inner loop to process one level at a time.
intro: Return level order traversal as list of lists.
talkTrack: Snapshot-size pattern reused in Rotting Oranges, Right Side View, Word Ladder. Master this template.

title: Clone Graph
shortSummary: DFS with HashMap<original,clone>. Create clone BEFORE recursing (handles cycles).
intro: Deep copy a connected undirected graph.
talkTrack: Create clone BEFORE recursing into neighbors — prevents infinite recursion on cycles.

title: Evaluate Reverse Polish Notation
shortSummary: Push numbers. On operator: pop two, compute, push result.
intro: Evaluate arithmetic expression in Reverse Polish Notation.
talkTrack: Pop order matters for non-commutative ops: first pop = right operand. For a-b: pop b, pop a, compute a-b.

title: Course Schedule
shortSummary: Directed graph cycle detection. DFS 3-color: unvisited(0), visiting(1), done(2).
intro: Given n courses and prerequisites, can you finish all courses? (Detect cycle in DAG.)
talkTrack: 3-color DFS: gray node (visiting) reached again = back edge = cycle. Also works with Kahn's BFS topological sort.

title: Implement Trie
shortSummary: TrieNode with children[26] and isEnd boolean. All ops traverse char by char.
intro: Implement a Trie with insert, search, startsWith.
talkTrack: Tries trade space for time. O(m) prefix search vs O(m×n) for sorted set. Critical for autocomplete.

title: Coin Change
shortSummary: dp[i]=min coins for amount i. dp[0]=0. dp[i]=min(dp[i-coin]+1) for each coin.
intro: Minimum number of coins to make target amount.
talkTrack: Initialize to amount+1 (not MAX_VALUE to avoid overflow on +1). dp[0]=0 is base case.

title: Product of Array Except Self
shortSummary: Left pass fills prefix products. Right pass multiplies by rolling suffix product.
intro: Return array where each element is product of all others. No division. O(n) time.
talkTrack: Left products stored in output. Right products accumulated in a single rolling variable — no extra array needed.

title: Min Stack
shortSummary: Two stacks: main + minStack. Push to minStack when val <= current min. Pop minStack when top removed.
intro: Stack supporting push, pop, top, getMin in O(1).
talkTrack: Use <= (not <) to handle duplicate mins. Pop minStack only when the removed value equals its top.

title: Validate Binary Search Tree
shortSummary: Pass (min,max) bounds. Each node must satisfy min < val < max.
intro: Determine if a binary tree is a valid BST.
edgeCases: Value equals Integer.MIN/MAX — use Long bounds
Duplicate values not allowed in BST
talkTrack: Common mistake: only comparing with parent. Correct: each node has a valid range from all ancestors.

title: Number of Islands
shortSummary: For each unvisited "1": DFS to mark all connected land as "0". Count DFS calls.
intro: Count islands in a 2D grid of "1" (land) and "0" (water).
talkTrack: Mutate grid in-place to avoid extra visited array. Each DFS call discovers one complete island.

title: Rotting Oranges
shortSummary: Multi-source BFS from all rotten oranges. BFS levels = minutes. Return -1 if fresh remain.
intro: Find minimum time for all fresh oranges to rot. -1 if impossible.
talkTrack: Multi-source BFS from all rotten simultaneously. Each level = 1 minute. If fresh>0 after BFS, unreachable.

title: Search in Rotated Sorted Array
shortSummary: One half is always sorted. Check if target falls in sorted half; narrow accordingly.
intro: Search target in rotated sorted array in O(log n).
talkTrack: In any rotation, one half is always sorted. Identify sorted half first, then check if target is in it.
followups: Find Min in Rotated Sorted Array?

title: Combination Sum
shortSummary: Backtrack: at each step try each candidate from start. Pass i (not i+1) to allow reuse.
intro: Find all unique combinations summing to target. Candidates can be reused.
talkTrack: Backtrack template: try, recurse, undo. Pass i (not i+1) = reuse allowed. Sort + break for pruning.

title: Permutations
shortSummary: Backtrack with used[] array. At each position try all unused elements.
intro: Return all permutations of distinct integers.
talkTrack: n! permutations. used[] prevents reuse. Difference from CombinationSum: no start index, always try all positions.

title: Merge Intervals
shortSummary: Sort by start. If current overlaps last merged (last.end >= curr.start), extend last.end.
intro: Merge all overlapping intervals.
talkTrack: Sort by start. Scan: if next starts before end of last merged, extend. Else start new group.

title: LCA of Binary Tree
shortSummary: Return node if it is p or q. LCA = node where both subtrees return non-null.
intro: Find LCA of two nodes in a general binary tree (not BST).
talkTrack: If both subtrees return non-null, root is LCA (split point). If only one returns non-null, both are in that subtree.

title: Time Based Key-Value Store
shortSummary: HashMap<key, TreeMap<ts,val>>. get() uses floorKey(timestamp).
intro: Design a time-based key-value store. get(key,ts) returns value at largest timestamp <= ts.
talkTrack: TreeMap.floorKey() gives largest timestamp <= query. Alternatively binary search on sorted list if timestamps guaranteed increasing.
followups: What if timestamps could be out of order?

title: Accounts Merge
shortSummary: Union-Find on emails. All emails in same account share root. Group by root, sort, prepend name.
intro: Merge accounts that share emails.
talkTrack: Union-Find on string keys. Union all emails in an account to the first email. Group by root.

title: Sort Colors
shortSummary: lo/mid/hi pointers. 0→swap with lo; 2→swap with hi (no mid++); 1→mid++.
intro: Sort array of 0s,1s,2s in-place, one pass.
talkTrack: Dutch National Flag. When we swap a 2 to hi, we don't increment mid because the swapped element is unknown.

title: Word Break
shortSummary: dp[i]=true if s[0..i) segmentable. Check all splits dp[j] && s[j..i) in dict.
intro: Can string s be segmented into dictionary words?
talkTrack: dp[i] = can first i chars be segmented. For each i, check all possible last words s[j..i).

title: Partition Equal Subset Sum
shortSummary: dp[j]=can we make sum j? Iterate j right-to-left to avoid reuse (0/1 knapsack).
intro: Can the array be split into two equal-sum subsets?
talkTrack: Reduce to subset-sum = total/2. Right-to-left iteration prevents using same number twice.

title: String to Integer (atoi)
shortSummary: Skip whitespace → handle sign → parse digits → clamp to INT range.
intro: Implement atoi: string to 32-bit signed integer with edge cases.
talkTrack: Walk states: whitespace→sign→digits→stop at non-digit. Use long to detect overflow.

title: Spiral Matrix
shortSummary: Four boundary pointers top/bottom/left/right. Traverse right→down→left→up, shrink.
intro: Return all elements of m×n matrix in spiral order.
talkTrack: Shrink boundary after each side. Check remaining boundaries before traversing bottom/left to avoid double-counting single rows.

title: Subsets
shortSummary: Backtrack: add current state to result at EVERY recursive call, not just at leaf.
intro: Return all subsets (power set) of distinct integers.
talkTrack: Add current subset at every node (not just leaves). Start index prevents duplicates.

title: Binary Tree Right Side View
shortSummary: BFS level-by-level. Last node polled per level is visible from right.
intro: Return values visible from the right side of a binary tree.
talkTrack: Level-order BFS template. Last polled in each level = rightmost. Reuses the size-snapshot pattern.

title: Longest Palindromic Substring
shortSummary: Expand around each center (odd and even). Track max expansion.
intro: Find the longest palindromic substring in s.
talkTrack: Expand-around-center O(n²) time O(1) space — simpler than Manacher's O(n). Right answer for interviews.

title: Unique Paths
shortSummary: dp[j] += dp[j-1]. First row all 1s. Or math: C(m+n-2, m-1).
intro: Count paths from top-left to bottom-right of m×n grid (right and down only).
talkTrack: dp[j] from previous iteration = "from above", dp[j-1] current = "from left".

title: Construct Binary Tree from Preorder and Inorder
shortSummary: preorder[0]=root. Find root in inorder to split left/right subtrees. Recurse.
intro: Build binary tree from preorder and inorder traversals.
talkTrack: Preorder: root first. Inorder tells us subtree sizes. HashMap for O(1) root lookup.

title: Container With Most Water
shortSummary: Move shorter side inward. Taller side can only benefit from a taller partner, not shorter width.
intro: Find two lines forming a container with max water.
talkTrack: Moving taller side can only decrease water (narrower + height same or less). Moving shorter side gives chance at improvement.

title: Letter Combinations of Phone Number
shortSummary: Backtrack: for each digit try each mapped letter, recurse on next digit.
intro: Return all letter combinations a phone number string could represent.
talkTrack: Classic backtrack: build string, backtrack by deleting last char. Branching factor 3 or 4.

title: Word Search
shortSummary: DFS+backtrack from each cell. Mark visited with # during DFS; restore on return.
intro: Determine if word exists as a path in the grid.
talkTrack: Mark visited BEFORE recursing, restore AFTER. Prevents using same cell twice in one path.

title: Find All Anagrams in a String
shortSummary: Fixed-size window of length p. Compare int[26] frequency arrays. O(1) comparison.
intro: Find all start indices of anagram substrings of p in s.
talkTrack: Fixed-size window. Arrays.equals on two int[26] is O(26)=O(1).

title: Minimum Height Trees
shortSummary: Leaf pruning: repeatedly remove degree-1 nodes until 1-2 remain. Those are MHT roots.
intro: Find roots of minimum height trees in undirected graph.
edgeCases: Star graph — center is only MHT root
talkTrack: Peel onion from outside in. Leaves never optimal. 1-2 remaining nodes are always the topological center.

title: Task Scheduler
shortSummary: Answer = max(tasks.length, (maxFreq-1)*(n+1) + countOfMaxFreq).
intro: Minimum time to finish all tasks with cooldown n between same tasks.
edgeCases: Many different types fill idle slots
talkTrack: (maxFreq-1) complete frames of n+1 slots + final partial frame. If tasks dense enough, no idle needed.
followups: How to actually schedule the tasks (not just count time)?

title: LRU Cache
shortSummary: HashMap + doubly-linked list. Map for O(1) lookup. DLL for O(1) insert/remove by recency.
intro: Design LRU cache with O(1) get and put.
edgeCases: Put existing key (update + move to front)
talkTrack: Java LinkedHashMap can do this in 5 lines, but interviewers want the manual DLL. Dummy head/tail eliminate null checks. Map stores key→Node for O(1) DLL pointer access.

title: Kth Smallest Element in BST
shortSummary: Inorder traversal visits BST in sorted order. Return kth element.
intro: Return the kth smallest element in a BST.
talkTrack: Iterative inorder stops as soon as k is reached — O(H+k) not O(n). Mention augmenting BST with subtree sizes for O(log n) if frequently modified.
followups: Augment BST for O(log n) kth queries?

title: Minimum Window Substring
shortSummary: Expand right until all t chars satisfied. Shrink left while valid. Track minimum window.
intro: Find minimum window substring of s containing all chars of t.
talkTrack: valid counter tracks unique chars fully satisfied. When valid==need.size(), window is valid; shrink from left to minimize.

title: Serialize and Deserialize Binary Tree
shortSummary: BFS serialization with "null" markers. Deserialization reconstructs level by level.
intro: Design serialize/deserialize for a binary tree.
talkTrack: BFS with null markers = deterministic reversible encoding. Preorder DFS with nulls also works.
followups: Why is preorder+inorder not enough (breaks with duplicates)?

title: Trapping Rain Water
shortSummary: Two pointers. Process side with smaller max. water[i] = min(maxL,maxR) - height[i].
intro: Compute total water trapped after raining on bar heights.
talkTrack: Process from side with smaller max because we KNOW the other side has at least that much. We can compute water contribution without knowing exact other max.

title: Find Median from Data Stream
shortSummary: Max-heap (lower half) + min-heap (upper half). Keep sizes balanced. Median = average of tops or top of larger.
intro: Design data structure for O(log n) add, O(1) findMedian.
talkTrack: Always push to lo first then rebalance to hi — ensures lo.peek() <= hi.peek(). lo size >= hi size.

title: Word Ladder
shortSummary: BFS where each step = one character change. Try all 26×L substitutions. Remove from set to avoid revisit.
intro: Find shortest transformation sequence from beginWord to endWord via wordList.
talkTrack: Model as graph: words = nodes, edges = single-char difference. BFS = shortest path. Remove from set as visited.

title: Basic Calculator
shortSummary: Stack saves (result,sign) on "(". Reset for inner expression. On ")" combine with saved outer state.
intro: Evaluate expression string with +, -, and parentheses.
talkTrack: On "(": save (result, sign) on stack, reset. On ")": combine inner result with saved outer state.

title: Maximum Profit in Job Scheduling
shortSummary: Sort by end time. dp[i]=max profit using first i jobs. Binary search for last non-conflicting job.
intro: Find maximum profit from non-overlapping jobs given start, end, profit arrays.
edgeCases: All jobs overlap — pick single highest profit
talkTrack: Sort by end time. dp[i] = max(skip = dp[i-1], take = dp[lastNonConflict] + profit[i]). Binary search finds rightmost job ending before current starts.

title: Merge k Sorted Lists
shortSummary: Min-heap of size k. Poll min, add to result, enqueue next node from that list.
intro: Merge k sorted linked lists into one sorted list.
talkTrack: Heap always holds at most k elements — O(log k) per poll/offer. Could also divide-and-conquer merging pairs.
followups: Find Median from k Sorted Arrays?

title: Largest Rectangle in Histogram
shortSummary: Monotonic increasing stack of indices. When shorter bar arrives, pop and compute area.
intro: Find largest rectangle area in histogram.
edgeCases: Decreasing sequence — all pop at end
talkTrack: Sentinel 0 at end flushes remaining bars. When shorter bar arrives, we know extent of all taller blocked bars. Width computed using stack peek as left boundary.
followups: Maximal Rectangle (2D — apply histogram per row)?

title: URL Shortening Service
shortSummary: Base62 encode a unique auto-increment ID → 7 char short code. Redis cache for reads. 301 vs 302 decision.
problemStatement: Design TinyURL: map long URLs to short codes and redirect at massive scale.
requirements: Shorten URL, redirect to original, custom aliases. 100:1 read:write ratio, <10ms redirect latency.
highLevelDesign:
Architecture:
Client → API Gateway → URL Service → PostgreSQL (writes)
                      ↓
                   Redis Cache (short_code → long_url)
                      ↓ (miss)
                   DB Read Replica

Short Code Generation:
  Auto-increment ID → base62 encode → 7 chars (62^7 = 3.5 trillion URLs)
  Or: MD5(long_url) → first 6 bytes → base62 → collision check

DB Schema:
  urls(id BIGINT PK, short_code VARCHAR(10) UNIQUE, long_url TEXT,
       user_id, created_at, expires_at)
  Index on short_code

Redirect Flow:
  Check Redis → hit (99%) return URL
  On miss: query DB, populate Redis (TTL=1hr), return URL
  Return 301 (client caches) or 302 (server tracks analytics)
scalingStrategy:
Reads: Redis cluster (shard by short_code hash)
Writes: Rate limit per user, async analytics writes to Kafka
Sharding: Shard urls table by short_code prefix
IDs: Twitter Snowflake for distributed unique ID generation
CDN: Cache 301 responses at edge
tradeoffs:
301 vs 302:
  301 Permanent: client caches → less load, no analytics tracking
  302 Temporary: every visit hits server → analytics possible, more load

Base62 vs MD5:
  Counter+Base62: sequential (guessable), no collision
  MD5 hash: non-sequential, collision possible (need resolution)
talkTrack: I clarify: do we need analytics? (→301 vs 302). Focus on Redis as critical for 100:1 ratio. Snowflake IDs for distributed ID generation.

title: API Rate Limiter
shortSummary: Token bucket (production standard) in Redis with Lua scripts for atomic check-and-decrement.
problemStatement: Restrict requests per user/IP in a time window across distributed servers.
requirements: <1ms overhead, horizontally scalable, fair limits, handle Redis failure gracefully.
highLevelDesign:
Algorithms:
1. Fixed Window Counter (simple, has boundary burst problem)
   Redis: INCR "limit:{user}:{minute}" + EXPIRE

2. Token Bucket (most common in production — AWS, Stripe use this)
   Bucket = N tokens, refilled at rate R/sec
   Redis: {tokens, last_refill_ts} stored per user
   Lua script: atomic check-and-decrement

3. Sliding Window Counter (best balance)
   Current + (previous × overlap fraction)
   Two counters in Redis

Architecture:
  Request → Rate Limiter Middleware → API Service
                   ↓
             Redis Cluster (user_id sharded)

Response Headers:
  X-RateLimit-Limit: 100
  X-RateLimit-Remaining: 42
  X-RateLimit-Reset: 1719820800
  HTTP 429 on violation
scalingStrategy:
Redis Cluster: shard by user_id
Lua scripts: atomic check+decrement prevents race conditions
Multi-tier: per IP (coarse), per user (per-account), per endpoint (fine)
Fail-open vs fail-closed: business decision on Redis failure
tradeoffs:
Strict vs Approximate:
  Fixed window: simple but 2x burst at window boundary
  Token bucket: controlled bursts, good UX
  Sliding window log: exact but high memory

Centralized (Redis, exact) vs Local (fast but N×limit worst case):
  Production: Redis for correctness
followups: Fail-open vs fail-closed on Redis outage?
talkTrack: Token bucket = production standard. Explain why fixed window has boundary burst problem. Redis Lua scripts for atomicity across distributed nodes.

title: Distributed Cache
shortSummary: Consistent hashing for sharding. LRU eviction. Cache-aside pattern. Handle stampede with mutex.
problemStatement: Design a distributed in-memory cache like Redis at scale.
highLevelDesign:
Core Components:
1. Consistent Hashing: virtual nodes minimize rehashing on add/remove
2. Replication: primary-replica per shard (async for perf)
3. Eviction: LRU (most common), LFU (for frequency skew), TTL-based

Cache Patterns:
  Cache-Aside (Lazy): app checks → miss → load DB → populate cache
  Write-Through: write to cache AND DB synchronously (consistent, slow writes)
  Write-Behind: write cache → async flush to DB (fast, risk of loss)
  Read-Through: cache auto-loads from DB on miss

Failure Modes:
  Cache Stampede: many misses simultaneously → DB overload
    → Fix: mutex/lock, probabilistic early refresh, request coalescing
  Hot Key: one key gets millions of req/s
    → Fix: replicate hot key to multiple nodes, local L1 cache
  Cache Penetration: key doesn't exist → DB every time
    → Fix: bloom filter, negative caching with short TTL
scalingStrategy:
Horizontal: add shards via consistent hashing
Read scaling: more replicas per shard
Hot spots: local in-process L1 in front of distributed cache
TTL jitter: add random offset to prevent thundering herd on expiry
tradeoffs:
Sync vs Async replication:
  Sync: consistent, higher write latency
  Async: fast, possible read-your-writes inconsistency

Write-Through vs Write-Behind vs Cache-Aside: depends on consistency vs perf needs
talkTrack: Structure around: consistent hashing (why it matters), the three write patterns, cache stampede as real production problem solved with distributed locks.

title: Key-Value Store
shortSummary: LSM-tree for fast writes. Consistent hashing for partitioning. Quorum R+W>N for strong consistency.
problemStatement: Design a distributed KV store like DynamoDB with tunable consistency.
highLevelDesign:
Storage Engine (LSM-Tree):
  Write: WAL → memtable (in-memory sorted) → SSTable flush
  Read: memtable → SSTables newest to oldest → Bloom filter skips
  Background: compaction removes old versions

Replication:
  Consistent hashing: key → N physical nodes
  Tunable quorum:
    R=1, W=1 → eventual consistency (fastest)
    R+W > N → strong consistency
    W=N → always consistent writes, slow

Conflict Resolution:
  Vector clocks: track causality
  LWW (Last Write Wins): simple, risk of data loss
  Application-level merge: Dynamo shopping cart approach
scalingStrategy:
Partitioning: consistent hashing with virtual nodes
Compaction: leveled (better reads) or tiered (better writes)
Anti-entropy: Merkle trees detect + repair diverged replicas
Gossip protocol: node state propagation without coordinator
tradeoffs:
CAP:
  CP (HBase): strong consistency, sacrifice availability
  AP (Cassandra): always available, eventual consistency

Leveled vs Tiered Compaction:
  Leveled: better read perf, more write amplification
  Tiered: better write perf, slower reads
talkTrack: Write path (WAL → memtable → SSTable) as foundation. R/W quorum tuning = CAP tradeoffs. Bloom filters = key optimization interviewers love.

title: API Gateway
shortSummary: Single entry point. Handles auth, rate limiting, routing, SSL termination, circuit breaking. Stateless — state in Redis.
problemStatement: Design an API Gateway for a microservices architecture.
highLevelDesign:
Core Responsibilities:
1. Request Routing: /api/orders/* → Order Service (via service registry)
2. Authentication: validate JWT/OAuth before forwarding
3. Rate Limiting: per user/IP/endpoint (state in Redis)
4. SSL Termination: HTTPS at gateway, HTTP internally
5. Load Balancing: distribute to healthy service instances
6. Circuit Breaker: fail fast when downstream is down
7. Request Transformation: versioning, schema mapping
8. Observability: inject request-id, log, trace

Architecture:
  Client → DNS → CDN → Load Balancer → API Gateway Cluster (stateless)
                                              ↓
                                   Service Registry (Consul/Eureka)
                                        ↓
                        Order Service | User Service | Payment Service

Request Pipeline:
  SSL termination → JWT validation → rate limit check
  → route resolve → request transform → forward → log → return
scalingStrategy:
Stateless gateway: all state (rate limits, sessions) in Redis
Multiple gateway instances behind LB
Hot config reload: route updates without restart
Health checks: gateway monitors upstream health continuously
tradeoffs:
Monolithic vs BFF (Backend for Frontend):
  Monolithic: simpler ops, one gateway for all clients
  BFF: tailored per client type (mobile/web), more flexible but complex

Sync vs Async:
  Most: synchronous passthrough
  Some routes: publish to Kafka for event-driven flows
followups: Service mesh vs API Gateway (Istio vs Kong)?
talkTrack: Gateway must be stateless itself — rate limit state lives in Redis, auth tokens verified cryptographically. Enables horizontal scaling.

title: Distributed Job Scheduler
shortSummary: Leader election (etcd/ZK). Jobs in DB with next_run_time. Worker claims with distributed lock. Idempotent execution.
problemStatement: Design a distributed cron-like scheduler that must not miss or double-fire jobs.
highLevelDesign:
Components:
1. Job Store (DB): jobs(id, cron_expr, next_run_time, status, payload)
2. Scheduler (Leader): polls for due jobs, publishes to Kafka
3. Workers: consume + execute jobs idempotently
4. Leader Election: ZooKeeper ephemeral nodes or etcd with Raft

Flow:
  Leader: SELECT * FROM jobs WHERE next_run_time <= NOW() FOR UPDATE
  Leader locks job → publishes to Kafka → Workers execute
  Worker updates next_run_time after completion

Distributed Lock:
  Redis SET job_id+ts NX PX 30000 (set-if-not-exists, 30s TTL)
  Fencing token: monotonically increasing token with each lock grant
  Resource rejects writes with stale (lower) token → prevents zombie writer

Exactly-once:
  At-least-once + idempotency key + idempotent job logic
scalingStrategy:
Leader: ZooKeeper or etcd Raft-based
Workers: auto-scale on queue depth
Job partitioning: shard by job_id for parallel scheduling
tradeoffs:
At-least-once (easier): idempotent jobs, re-run on failure
Exactly-once (hard): requires dedup table or distributed transaction

Push to queue vs workers poll DB directly:
  Queue: decoupled, scalable
  Direct poll: simpler but thundering herd at scale
talkTrack: Hardest part is exactly-once. Fencing tokens prevent slow worker executing after losing its lock. Idempotency is the safety net.

title: Distributed Lock Manager
shortSummary: Strong guarantees: etcd/ZK with Raft. Weaker: Redlock (5 Redis nodes). Always include fencing tokens.
problemStatement: Ensure only one process holds a lock at a time across distributed nodes.
highLevelDesign:
Options (strongest to weakest):

1. Consensus-based (etcd/ZooKeeper) — most reliable:
   etcd: lease-based locks, Raft consensus
   ZK: ephemeral nodes auto-released on disconnect
   Reliable, operationally complex

2. Redlock (Redis multi-node):
   Acquire lock on majority of N=5 Redis nodes
   SET key uuid NX PX ttl_ms
   Release: check UUID matches → DEL (Lua script, atomic)
   Practical but has timing edge cases under clock skew

3. Database-based (simple):
   INSERT INTO locks(name, holder, expires_at)
   DB becomes bottleneck at scale

Fencing Token Pattern:
  Lock service returns monotonically increasing token
  Token included in writes to protected resource
  Resource rejects writes with stale token → prevents zombie writers
tradeoffs:
Safety vs Liveness:
  Raft: never grants 2 locks simultaneously, may be unavailable
  Redlock: practical tradeoff, edge cases under network partition

TTL too short: lock expires while holder still working
TTL too long: other clients wait too long after holder crash
followups: Why is single Redis node insufficient?
talkTrack: Frame around: what guarantees are needed? Best effort → Redis. Critical (financial) → etcd with Raft. Always mention fencing tokens: clock-based TTLs alone are not safe.

title: Metrics and Monitoring System
shortSummary: Time-series DB (write-optimized, columnar). Pull-based scraping (Prometheus) or push agents. Alert on threshold breach.
problemStatement: Design a metrics collection and monitoring system like Datadog or Prometheus.
highLevelDesign:
Components:
1. Agents: run on each host, collect system/app metrics
2. Aggregation: batch, downsample, forward
3. TSDB (Time-Series DB): columnar, LSM-based, optimized for time-ordered writes
   Data model: (metric_name, tags, timestamp, value)
4. Query Engine: PromQL-style range queries, aggregations
5. Alerting: evaluate rules periodically, notify PagerDuty/Slack
6. Dashboard: Grafana-style visualization

Data Flow (Pull / Prometheus):
  TSDB ← Scraper ← /metrics endpoints

Data Flow (Push / Datadog):
  Services → Agent → Aggregation → TSDB

Metric Types:
  Counter: monotonically increasing (request count)
  Gauge: point-in-time (CPU %)
  Histogram: distribution (latency buckets)
scalingStrategy:
Writes: shard by metric name hash
Retention: 1s data for 24h, 1m rollups for 30d, 1h for 1yr
Hot metrics: in-memory ring buffer for last 5 min
Cardinality: limit tag combinations (metric explosion problem)
tradeoffs:
Pull vs Push:
  Pull (Prometheus): simple, one scraper, clear collection scope
  Push (Datadog): works behind NAT, good for short-lived jobs
followups: Handle metric loss during agent downtime?
talkTrack: Why time-series differs from relational: write-heavy, always append, rarely update, downsampled over time. Cardinality explosion (every tag combo = new series) is the key scaling challenge.

title: Notification System
shortSummary: Event → Kafka → Notification Router → channel workers (FCM/APNs/Email/SMS). Idempotency key prevents duplication.
problemStatement: Multi-channel notification system (push, email, SMS, in-app) at scale.
highLevelDesign:
Architecture:
  Event Sources → Kafka → Notification Router
                               ↓         ↓          ↓         ↓
                          Push Worker  Email   SMS Worker  In-App
                          (FCM/APNs) (SendGrid) (Twilio)   (DB)

Components:
1. Kafka: buffer events, ordered delivery per user_id partition
2. User Preferences Service: which channels per user (DB)
3. Router: determines channels, creates per-channel messages
4. Channel Workers: async send via external providers
5. Deduplication: idempotency_key prevents duplicate sends on retry
6. Retry Queue: failed sends → delay queue with exponential backoff

Rules:
  NEVER slow main business transaction waiting for notification
  Always async: order created → Kafka event → workers send
scalingStrategy:
Kafka partitioning: by user_id for ordered delivery
Workers: auto-scale per channel on queue depth
Rate limits: respect external provider limits (FCM: 1M/min)
Batching: group email digests, batch push per device
tradeoffs:
At-most-once (OK for non-critical) vs At-least-once + idempotent (OTP, payments)
Notification timing: respect user timezone for non-urgent
talkTrack: Stress that notifications must never block the main transaction. Kafka decouples. Idempotency key in payload prevents duplicate sends on retry.

title: Live Chat / Twitch Chat
shortSummary: WebSockets per client. Redis Pub/Sub per channel fans out to all servers. Lossy delivery OK at Twitch scale.
problemStatement: Live chat system handling 100K+ concurrent viewers per channel.
highLevelDesign:
Architecture:
  Client ←WebSocket→ Chat Server → Redis Pub/Sub (channel topic)
                          ↓                ↓
                  Cassandra (history)  All Chat Servers subscribed
                                        broadcast to their clients

Components:
1. WebSocket Servers: stateful, hold N connections each
2. Message Bus: Redis Pub/Sub or Kafka per channel
3. Message Store: Cassandra (partition by channel_id, sort by timestamp)
4. Presence: Redis SET per channel, expire on disconnect
5. Rate Limiter: anti-spam, N msg/user/min

Fan-out at Scale:
  Channel with 1M viewers → hierarchical fan-out
  Message → Kafka → Region relays → Chat servers → clients

Delivery Semantics:
  Twitch: intentionally lossy at extreme scale (OK to drop some)
  WhatsApp: must deliver every message (very different design)
scalingStrategy:
10K WebSocket connections per server → many servers
Session affinity: user routed to same server (Redis mapping)
Geographic: regional chat servers for latency
Backpressure: drop oldest on buffer overflow (Twitch approach)
tradeoffs:
WebSocket vs SSE vs Long-polling:
  WebSocket: full-duplex, best for bidirectional
  SSE: server→client only, simpler
  Long-polling: fallback, high overhead

Lossy vs Lossless: core design fork based on use case
talkTrack: Always discuss fan-out problem explicitly. For Twitch scale, dropping messages is acceptable. For WhatsApp, every message must be acknowledged. This fork drives the entire design.

title: Google Docs Collaborative Editing
shortSummary: Operational Transforms (OT) for conflict-free concurrent edits. WebSocket for real-time sync. Op log in Cassandra.
problemStatement: Real-time collaborative document editor where multiple users edit simultaneously.
highLevelDesign:
Core Challenge: Concurrent edits conflict
  User A at position 5 inserts "hello"
  User B at position 5 deletes a char (simultaneously)
  → Without resolution: corrupted document

Solution: Operational Transforms (OT) — used by Google Docs
  Server maintains operation log
  Client sends ops: Insert(pos=5, "hello")
  Server transforms concurrent ops against each other
  All clients converge to same state

Architecture:
  Client A ←WebSocket→ Doc Server ←→ OT Engine ←→ Cassandra (op log)
  Client B ←WebSocket→      ↑                     Redis (current state)
                       Pub/Sub (Kafka)

Cursor broadcasts: send cursor positions to all clients (low priority, high frequency)
Autosave: client sends ops every 100ms; full snapshot every 30s
scalingStrategy:
Presence: WebSocket session count per doc
Conflict resolution: server = single source of truth (OT)
Large docs: chunk into sections, lock at section level
Offline edits: queue locally, apply on reconnect with OT
tradeoffs:
OT vs CRDT:
  OT: requires central server, Google Docs uses it, well-understood
  CRDT: peer-to-peer capable, no server needed, complex, larger data

Optimistic (Google): apply locally first, resolve on server
Pessimistic: lock doc (terrible UX)
followups: How does undo/redo work in collaborative editor?
talkTrack: Start with the problem: what happens when two users edit same position? OT is the more interview-friendly answer. Mention CRDTs as modern alternative.

title: Typeahead / Autocomplete
shortSummary: Redis sorted set for prefix lookup (ZRANGEBYLEX). Offline pipeline computes top-K per prefix. Sub-100ms SLA.
problemStatement: Typeahead search suggestion responding in <100ms.
highLevelDesign:
Data Structures:
Option 1: Trie
  Each node = prefix, stores top-K suggestions pre-computed
  Distributed: shard by first 2 chars of prefix

Option 2: Redis Sorted Set (simpler distributed deployment)
  ZADD prefix:ana → "analytics" with score=frequency
  ZRANGEBYLEX for prefix range: O(log N + k)

Architecture:
  User types → Frontend debounce (300ms) → CDN Cache (common prefixes)
  → Typeahead Service → Redis → Ranking Service → Response

Offline Pipeline (updates suggestions):
  Search logs → Spark/Flink → compute top-K per prefix → Redis
scalingStrategy:
CDN: cache top 1000 common prefixes at edge
Updates: async batch pipeline, not real-time (1hr lag OK)
Personalization: blend global frequency with user history
tradeoffs:
Accuracy vs Latency:
  Global frequency only: fast, not personalized
  Real-time personalization: complex, higher latency
  Practical: global first, blend personal in 2nd request
talkTrack: Data structure first (Trie vs Redis sorted set), then update frequency (real-time vs batch), then personalization as follow-up layer.

title: YouTube / Netflix
shortSummary: Upload → transcode multiple resolutions → S3 → CDN → adaptive bitrate streaming (HLS/DASH).
problemStatement: Video streaming platform at global scale.
highLevelDesign:
Upload & Processing Pipeline:
  Upload → S3 (raw) → Transcoding Queue (Kafka) → FFmpeg Workers
  → Multiple resolutions: 360p, 480p, 720p, 1080p, 4K
  → Segment into 2-6s chunks for HLS/DASH
  → Push segments to CDN

Streaming:
  Client measures bandwidth → requests quality level
  HLS: m3u8 manifest → chunked .ts files
  Player switches quality between segments (Adaptive Bitrate)

Architecture:
  Upload → API → S3 → Kafka → Transcoding Workers → CDN
  Metadata: Cassandra (video_id, title, views, chunk_manifest)
  Comments: Cassandra (partition by video_id)

View Count:
  Approximate: HyperLogLog, not exact per-request DB increment
scalingStrategy:
CDN: 99% of bandwidth from edge nodes
Object storage: S3/GCS with geo-replication
Video metadata: Cassandra sharded by video_id
Recommendation: offline ML pipeline → feature store → online serving
tradeoffs:
Push vs Pull CDN:
  Push (Netflix Open Connect): pre-populate on upload
  Pull (YouTube): CDN fetches on first request, caches

Live vs VOD:
  VOD: pre-transcode all qualities
  Live: real-time transcode, short segments (2s), high infra cost
talkTrack: Three tiers: storage (S3), processing (transcoding workers), delivery (CDN). Transcoding pipeline often underweighted — spend time on async queue-based approach.

title: Dropbox
shortSummary: Chunk files into 4MB blocks by content hash. Upload only new chunks. Metadata service tracks chunk lists.
problemStatement: File storage and sync system like Dropbox.
highLevelDesign:
Core Innovations:
1. Chunking: split files into 4MB fixed-size or variable-size (Rabin fingerprint) chunks
2. Content-addressed storage: chunk_id = SHA-256(chunk) → automatic deduplication
3. Delta sync: only re-upload changed chunks on file modification

Architecture:
  Client SDK → Block Server (upload chunks) → S3 (block store)
  Client SDK → Metadata Server → DB (file → chunk list mapping)
  Client SDK → Notification Server (WebSocket) ← real-time sync events

Upload Flow:
  1. Split file into chunks, compute hashes
  2. Ask Metadata Server: which chunks are new?
  3. Upload only new chunks to Block Server
  4. Commit metadata (filename, chunks[], version) to Metadata Server
  5. Notify other clients via WebSocket

Conflict Resolution:
  "Copy on conflict": save both versions (Dropbox's approach)
  LWW: simpler, risks data loss
scalingStrategy:
Block Store: S3 with regional replication
Metadata DB: PostgreSQL sharded by user_id
Dedup: typically 50-80% storage savings
Sync notification: WebSocket per client, Kafka for team shares
tradeoffs:
Fixed vs Variable Chunking:
  Fixed: simpler, but insert at start shifts all chunk boundaries
  Variable (Rabin): better dedup on similar files, more complex
talkTrack: Lead with chunking + content-addressed storage as the two key innovations. Hash-based dedup is the most important concept.

title: Gmail
shortSummary: Bigtable-like wide-row store per user. Full-text search index (Elasticsearch). Thread by References header. Attachments in S3.
problemStatement: Webmail at hyperscale with search, threading, attachments, and spam filtering.
highLevelDesign:
Data Model (Bigtable wide-row):
  Row key: user_id + reverse_timestamp (recent first)
  Columns: subject, body, from, to, labels, thread_id, is_read

Threading:
  Thread = group sharing References/In-Reply-To headers
  thread_id assigned on first email

Attachments:
  Stored in S3, email stores reference only
  Dedup: same attachment to multiple recipients = one stored copy

Search:
  Per-user Elasticsearch index (not global)
  Index: from, to, subject, body, labels, date
  Sorted by relevance × recency

Labels vs Folders:
  Gmail labels: one email in multiple "folders"
  Traditional folders: hierarchical, less flexible
scalingStrategy:
Storage: ~1M emails/user × 50KB = 50GB/user × 1B users = 50 exabytes
Search: per-user index shard
Spam: ML pipeline, reputation scoring, DKIM/SPF/DMARC validation
tradeoffs:
Push vs IMAP (poll):
  Push (WebSocket): instant, always-on connection
  IMAP: polling, simpler clients, industry standard
talkTrack: Bigtable wide-row model enables fast "inbox since timestamp" or "emails in this thread" without table scans.

title: Uber Backend
shortSummary: Driver location in Redis (GEOADD). GEORADIUS for nearby drivers. Trip state machine in PostgreSQL. WebSocket for tracking.
problemStatement: Rider-driver matching, real-time location tracking, trip management, surge pricing.
highLevelDesign:
Core Services:
1. Location Service:
   Drivers update location every 3-5s
   Redis GEOADD: stores lat/lng per driver_id
   GEORADIUS: "find all drivers within 2km of pickup"

2. Dispatch (Matching):
   On rider request: query nearby drivers
   Score each: distance + ETA + rating
   Offer to best driver (30s timeout), cascade to next on reject

3. Trip State Machine (PostgreSQL):
   REQUESTED → DRIVER_ACCEPTED → ARRIVING → IN_PROGRESS → COMPLETED
   Payment triggered on COMPLETED

4. Real-time Tracking:
   Rider subscribes to driver location via WebSocket
   Driver → Location Service → fan-out to rider's connection

5. Surge Pricing:
   Compare supply (available) vs demand (requests) per geohash cell
   surge_multiplier = demand/supply, capped at 2-3x
   Recomputed every 1-5 minutes per city zone
scalingStrategy:
Location: Redis Cluster, TTL 30s per driver (auto-removes offline)
Matching: stateless, scale horizontally
Trip events: Kafka for analytics
City isolation: separate services per city
tradeoffs:
Geospatial indexing: H3 (Uber's hex grid) vs Geohash vs S2
  H3: uniform area, best for demand analysis
  Geohash: rectangular, Redis native support

Centralized vs Distributed matching: global optimal vs resilient
talkTrack: Lead with location tracking architecture (most unique). Then dispatch flow, then trip state machine. Surge pricing = supply/demand ratio per geographic cell.

title: Airbnb
shortSummary: Elasticsearch for geo+availability search. Calendar locks for availability. Hold → payment → confirm booking flow.
problemStatement: Two-sided marketplace for property search, booking, and reviews.
highLevelDesign:
Core Services:
1. Property: CRUD, Elasticsearch geo indexing
2. Search: filter by location, dates, guests, price, amenities
3. Availability Calendar: per-property date blocking
4. Booking: hold → payment → confirm flow
5. Reviews: post-stay ratings

Booking Flow (critical consistency):
  1. User selects dates → 15-min HOLD (lock calendar)
  2. User confirms → payment auth
  3. Payment success → confirm booking, notify host
  4. Payment fail / timeout → release hold

Availability: DB table: availability(property_id, date, status)
  Optimistic locking prevents double booking
scalingStrategy:
Search: Elasticsearch with geo + date range filter
Availability: Redis for holds, DB for committed bookings
Pricing: base + dynamic demand-based algorithm
tradeoffs:
Real-time vs near-real-time availability:
  Real-time: PostgreSQL SELECT FOR UPDATE (strong consistency)
  Near-real-time: Redis cache + periodic sync

Instant book vs request-to-book: UX vs host control
talkTrack: Availability calendar is the hardest consistency problem. Hold → payment → confirm flow with explicit timeout handling prevents double bookings.

title: Payment System
shortSummary: Double-entry ledger. Idempotency key prevents duplicate charges. PSP integration with webhook retry. PostgreSQL for ACID.
problemStatement: Process transactions reliably with no money lost or double-charged.
highLevelDesign:
Core Principles:
1. Double-entry bookkeeping:
   Every debit = corresponding credit
   Sum of all ledger entries = 0 always

2. Idempotency:
   Every request has unique idempotency_key
   Same key again → return original response (don't re-charge)
   Table: idempotency(key, response, created_at, expires_at)

3. Exactly-once via "at-least-once + idempotent":
   Network can drop responses → retry safe with idempotency key

State Machine:
  PENDING → PROCESSING → COMPLETED / FAILED / CANCELLED

Tables:
  transactions(id, from_acct, to_acct, amount, currency, status, idempotency_key)
  account_balances(account_id, balance, version) — optimistic locking

PSP Integration:
  API → Stripe/PayPal → webhook callback on completion
  Webhook retry with dedup to handle duplicate callbacks
scalingStrategy:
Read replicas for balance queries
Sharding: by account_id (full history on one shard)
Async: webhook notification after sync confirmation
Reconciliation: nightly job vs PSP records
tradeoffs:
SQL vs NoSQL:
  Payments need ACID → PostgreSQL/MySQL
  NoSQL works but loses multi-row ACID guarantees

Sync vs Async:
  Sync: user waits (up to 30s), simpler
  Async: instant ACK, processed in background, complex refunds
talkTrack: Idempotency is the most critical property — "charge once, no matter how many retries." Walk through: what if network fails after Stripe charges but before we get 200 response?

title: Flash Sale System
shortSummary: Pre-warm inventory in Redis. Queue incoming requests. Atomic Redis DECR for inventory. Async order creation.
problemStatement: Handle 1M concurrent buyers for 10K items without oversell.
highLevelDesign:
Core Challenge:
  10K items, 1M users hit Buy at 12:00:00.000
  Must not oversell; DB cannot handle 1M concurrent writes

Solution:
1. Pre-warm inventory: SET flash_sale:{id}:qty 10000
2. Queue incoming requests: User → Queue → Kafka → Order Processor
   Return: "You're in queue, position #5234"
3. Atomic inventory deduction:
   DECR flash_sale:{id}:qty
   If result >= 0: allow purchase
   If result < 0: INCR back (restore), return "Sold Out"
4. Async order creation:
   On Redis success → create order in DB async
   Send payment link via notification

Race Condition Prevention:
  NAIVE: read qty, check > 0, decrement → RACE CONDITION
  CORRECT: Redis DECR is atomic → no race
scalingStrategy:
Rate limiting: 1 req/user/second (anti-bot)
CDN: serve static sale page from CDN, no origin hit
Pre-compute: all sale data ready before event start
DB writes: async, batched, post-sale
tradeoffs:
Queue vs Direct:
  Queue: position feedback, DB protection, complex
  Direct: simpler, DB dies at 1M concurrent

Redis DECR atomicity eliminates need for distributed lock
Failure: Redis crash between DECR and order → Redis AOF persistence needed
followups: Payment timeout cancels order → restore inventory?
talkTrack: Illustrate race condition: two threads both see qty=1, both proceed → oversell. Redis DECR is atomic — eliminates this.

title: Amazon S3
shortSummary: Erasure coding across AZs (50% overhead vs 200% for 3x replication). Metadata in distributed DB. Multi-part upload.
problemStatement: Globally distributed object storage with 11 nines of durability.
highLevelDesign:
Architecture:
  Client → API Service → Metadata Service → Metadata DB (DynamoDB-like)
                                 ↓
                          Storage Nodes (commodity hardware, partitioned)

Erasure Coding:
  Split object into k data + m parity chunks
  Can reconstruct from any k chunks (lose any m)
  Example: 8+4 → 50% overhead vs 3x replication = 200% overhead

Geo-replication:
  Copies in 3+ AZs for 11 nines durability

Operations:
  PUT: assign storage nodes → parallel writes → metadata commit (atomic)
  GET: lookup metadata → fetch k chunks → reconstruct → stream

Multi-part Upload:
  Large files: 5MB-5GB parts uploaded in parallel
  Parts assembled on completion → single atomic PUT

Metadata:
  objects(bucket, key, version_id, etag, size, storage_class, chunks_metadata)
scalingStrategy:
Consistent hashing: object → storage nodes
Caching: metadata cache + CDN for frequent objects
Storage tiers: Standard → IA → Glacier (cost vs latency)
Background repair: continuously check and restore degraded objects
tradeoffs:
Erasure Coding vs Replication:
  Replication (3x): 200% overhead, fast reads, any 1 copy works
  Erasure (8+4): 50% overhead, reconstruction cost, less redundant

Consistency: S3 now provides strong read-after-write for PUTs
talkTrack: Erasure coding = key to 11 nines without 3x storage cost. Metadata layer = single source of truth for object location. Multi-part upload = the mechanism for large file reliability.

title: Code Judge System
shortSummary: Sandboxed Docker containers with cgroup resource limits. Job queue. Output diff vs expected. Pre-warmed container pool.
problemStatement: Safely execute untrusted user code like LeetCode.
highLevelDesign:
Core Challenge: Run arbitrary untrusted code safely

Architecture:
  User submits → API Server → Kafka (job queue) → Judge Worker
                                                         ↓
                                            Isolated Container (Docker/gVisor)
                                                         ↓
                                        Execute with limits:
                                          CPU: 2 seconds
                                          Memory: 256MB
                                          Network: DISABLED
                                          Filesystem: read-only + tmpfs
                                                         ↓
                                        Diff output vs expected → Pass/Fail
                                                         ↓
                                        Result → DB → WebSocket → User

Sandbox Constraints:
  seccomp: whitelist allowed syscalls
  cgroup: limit CPU and memory
  No network: user code cannot make external calls
  Kill container after N seconds (time bomb)

Test Cases:
  Hidden test cases in S3
  Fetched per problem by judge worker
  Byte-for-byte diff vs expected output
scalingStrategy:
Worker pool: auto-scale on queue depth
Container reuse: pool of pre-warmed containers per language
Result cache: cache correct solutions to skip re-execution
Parallel: multiple test cases simultaneously in container
tradeoffs:
Container vs VM:
  Container: faster startup (~100ms), shared kernel
  VM (gVisor/Firecracker): stronger isolation, AWS Lambda uses Firecracker
  Production judge: gVisor or Firecracker for security
talkTrack: Focus on sandbox security first: while(true){} → killed by cgroup time limit; rm -rf / → read-only filesystem. Container isolation with seccomp + cgroups.

title: ChatGPT-style System
shortSummary: GPU cluster for inference. KV-cache per session. Stream tokens via SSE. Session affinity to same GPU worker.
problemStatement: Backend infrastructure for a conversational AI system at scale.
highLevelDesign:
Core Challenges:
  LLM inference is GPU-bound and stateful (conversation context)

Architecture:
  User → API Gateway → Session Service → Inference Router → GPU Workers
                             ↓                                    ↓
                    Redis (conversation history)          KV-Cache per request
                             ↓
                    Streaming SSE → Client (tokens appear as generated)

LLM Inference Flow:
  1. Receive message + conversation history from Redis
  2. Tokenize input (TikToken)
  3. Load context into GPU (KV-cache from previous tokens)
  4. Autoregressive generation: one token at a time
  5. Stream each token to client via Server-Sent Events (SSE)
  6. On completion: store response + history in Redis

KV-Cache:
  Attention K/V matrices cached per token → avoids recomputing
  Memory: ~1-3GB per conversation for 7B model
  Session affinity: route same conversation to same GPU worker

GPU Scheduling:
  Continuous batching: dynamically batch multiple requests on same GPU
  Quantization: INT8/INT4 to fit more requests per GPU
scalingStrategy:
GPU cluster: A100/H100, 8-80 GPUs per node
Horizontal: different conversations to different workers
Large models: tensor parallelism across multiple GPUs
Prefix caching: cache KV for common system prompts
tradeoffs:
Greedy vs Sampling:
  Greedy: fast, deterministic, less creative
  Temperature sampling: creative, non-deterministic

Context Length vs Cost:
  Longer history = more GPU compute per token
  Solutions: summarize old context, sliding window, memory compression
talkTrack: This is an inference infrastructure problem, not model training. Two unique challenges: (1) streaming token-by-token, (2) managing GPU memory across concurrent sessions with KV-cache.
